vulnerability.title: Ensure the Default Bash Umask is Set Correctly
vulnerability.description: To ensure the default umask for users of the Bash shell is set properly, add or correct the umask setting in /etc/bashrc to read as follows: umask
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q bash; }; then var_accounts_user_umask='' grep -q "^[^#]*\bumask" /etc/bashrc && \ sed -i -E -e "s/^([^#]*\bumask)[[:space:]]+[[:digit:]]+/\1 $var_accounts_user_umask/g" /etc/bashrc if ! [ $? -eq 0 ]; then echo "umask $var_accounts_user_umask" >> /etc/bashrc fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only edits /etc/bashrc to set a standard umask value, which is a simple text substitution and does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. The change is reversible and unlikely to cause lockout.

execution_details.stdout:
# /etc/bashrc

# System wide functions and aliases
# Environment stuff goes in /etc/profile

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

# Prevent doublesourcing
if [ -z "$BASHRCSOURCED" ]; then
  BASHRCSOURCED="Y"

  # are we an interactive shell?
  if [ "$PS1" ]; then
    if [ -z "$PROMPT_COMMAND" ]; then
      case $TERM in
      xterm*|vte*)
        if [ -e /etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-xterm ]; then
            PROMPT_COMMAND=/etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-xterm
        else
            PROMPT_COMMAND='printf "\033]0;%s@%s:%s\007" "${USER}" "${HOSTNAME%%.*}" "${PWD/#$HOME/\~}"'
        fi
        ;;
      screen*)
        if [ -e /etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-screen ]; then
            PROMPT_COMMAND=/etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-screen
        else
            PROMPT_COMMAND='printf "\033k%s@%s:%s\033\\" "${USER}" "${HOSTNAME%%.*}" "${PWD/#$HOME/\~}"'
        fi
        ;;
      *)
        [ -e /etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-default ] && PROMPT_COMMAND=/etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-default
        ;;
      esac
    fi
    # Turn on parallel history
    shopt -s histappend
    history -a
    # Turn on checkwinsize
    shopt -s checkwinsize
    [ "$PS1" = "\\s-\\v\\\$ " ] && PS1="[\u@\h \W]\\$ "
    # You might want to have e.g. tty in prompt (e.g. more virtual machines)
    # and console windows
    # If you want to do so, just add e.g.
    # if [ "$PS1" ]; then
    #   PS1="[\u@\h:\l \W]\\$ "
    # fi
    # to your custom modification shell script in /etc/profile.d/ directory
  fi

  if ! shopt -q login_shell ; then # We're not a login shell
    # Need to redefine pathmunge, it gets undefined at the end of /etc/profile
    pathmunge () {
        case ":${PATH}:" in
            *:"$1":*)
                ;;
            *)
                if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                    PATH=$PATH:$1
                else
                    PATH=$1:$PATH
                fi
        esac
    }

    # Set default umask for non-login shell only if it is set to 0
    [ `umask` -eq 0 ] && umask 022

    SHELL=/bin/bash
    # Only display echos from profile.d scripts if we are no login shell
    # and interactive - otherwise just process them to set envvars
    for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh; do
        if [ -r "$i" ]; then
            if [ "$PS1" ]; then
                . "$i"
            else
                . "$i" >/dev/null
            fi
        fi
    done

    unset i
    unset -f pathmunge
  fi

fi
# vim:ts=4:sw=4

# /etc/bashrc

# System wide functions and aliases
# Environment stuff goes in /etc/profile

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

# Prevent doublesourcing
if [ -z "$BASHRCSOURCED" ]; then
  BASHRCSOURCED="Y"

  # are we an interactive shell?
  if [ "$PS1" ]; then
    if [ -z "$PROMPT_COMMAND" ]; then
      case $TERM in
      xterm*|vte*)
        if [ -e /etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-xterm ]; then
            PROMPT_COMMAND=/etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-xterm
        else
            PROMPT_COMMAND='printf "\033]0;%s@%s:%s\007" "${USER}" "${HOSTNAME%%.*}" "${PWD/#$HOME/\~}"'
        fi
        ;;
      screen*)
        if [ -e /etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-screen ]; then
            PROMPT_COMMAND=/etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-screen
        else
            PROMPT_COMMAND='printf "\033k%s@%s:%s\033\\" "${USER}" "${HOSTNAME%%.*}" "${PWD/#$HOME/\~}"'
        fi
        ;;
      *)
        [ -e /etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-default ] && PROMPT_COMMAND=/etc/sysconfig/bash-prompt-default
        ;;
      esac
    fi
    # Turn on parallel history
    shopt -s histappend
    history -a
    # Turn on checkwinsize
    shopt -s checkwinsize
    [ "$PS1" = "\\s-\\v\\\$ " ] && PS1="[\u@\h \W]\\$ "
    # You might want to have e.g. tty in prompt (e.g. more virtual machines)
    # and console windows
    # If you want to do so, just add e.g.
    # if [ "$PS1" ]; then
    #   PS1="[\u@\h:\l \W]\\$ "
    # fi
    # to your custom modification shell script in /etc/profile.d/ directory
  fi

  if ! shopt -q login_shell ; then # We're not a login shell
    # Need to redefine pathmunge, it gets undefined at the end of /etc/profile
    pathmunge () {
        case ":${PATH}:" in
            *:"$1":*)
                ;;
            *)
                if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                    PATH=$PATH:$1
                else
                    PATH=$1:$PATH
                fi
        esac
    }

    # Set default umask for non-login shell only if it is set to 0
    [ `umask` -eq 0 ] && umask 022

    SHELL=/bin/bash
    # Only display echos from profile.d scripts if we are no login shell
    # and interactive - otherwise just process them to set envvars
    for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh; do
        if [ -r "$i" ]; then
            if [ "$PS1" ]; then
                . "$i"
            else
                . "$i" >/dev/null
            fi
        fi
    done

    unset i
    unset -f pathmunge
  fi

fi
# vim:ts=4:sw=4
umask 027

llm_verdict.message:
The remediation has been applied.

**File Modified:** `/etc/bashrc`  
**Change Made:** Added the line `umask 027` at the end of the file.

**Result:** The file now ends with:

```bash
# vim:ts=4:sw=4
umask 027
```

This sets the default Bash umask to the secure value of 027, satisfying the **accounts_umask_etc_bashrc** rule. The next verification scan should report the finding as resolved. If you need any further checks or additional changes, just let me know!

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
The profile lacks a specific umask value; the remediation text implies adding “umask 027”. The existing /etc/bashrc has a conditional umask 022, but no unconditional line. To avoid duplication, append “umask 027” at the end of the file (or after the conditional block). Use a check for an existing umask line before appending. This ensures the default umask is set to 027 as intended.

I verify that no existing non‑comment line starts with “umask”. Using `grep -E '^[[:space:]]*umask'` on `/etc/bashrc` returns nothing because the only “umask” appears inside a conditional (`[ \`umask\` -eq 0 ] && umask 022`). Since that line is not a direct `umask` directive, I can safely append a new `umask` line at the end of the file.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will set the default Bash umask to 027 by appending a line to /etc/bashrc, which will resolve the vulnerability. However, it is not idempotent and may create duplicate umask entries. Adding a check for existing umask settings and using a replace operation would be more robust.
concerns: May create duplicate umask entries if a umask line already exists.
Does not verify that Bash is installed or that the file is writable.
suggested_improvements: Check for an existing umask line with grep and replace it using sed instead of blindly appending.
Add a conditional to only append if no umask line is present.
Create a backup of /etc/bashrc before editing.
Verify that the Bash package is installed and that the script has appropriate permissions.
